# Digital Construction and Building Information Modeling

Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) is a widely adopted open standard for representing building data in the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) sector. IFC data is inherently object-oriented, encoding building elements through typed entities with rich attribute sets and explicit relationships—structural containment, spatial adjacency, material association, and system connectivity among them. This structure maps naturally to a labeled property graph model, where building components become nodes with domain-specific properties (e.g., dimensions, material grades, thermal coefficients) and their interrelationships become typed, directed edges. The resulting graph serves as a queryable digital twin of a physical building, encoding not just geometric form but the full semantic richness of the engineering model.

Graph databases are increasingly adopted in infrastructure and construction domains precisely because they preserve the relational semantics that tabular or document-based representations flatten or lose. An IFC-derived property graph can represent, for example, that a particular wall instance is contained within a specific storey, bounded by two spaces, composed of a layered material set, and penetrated by an opening hosting a door—all as traversable graph paths rather than disconnected table joins. This makes graph-native query languages well suited for answering engineering questions that inherently involve multi-hop traversal across building hierarchies and component assemblies.

Applying natural language interfaces to IFC-based graph data enables non-expert users—facility managers, building operators, regulatory inspectors—to access complex structured technical data without requiring knowledge of either the IFC schema or graph query syntax. Evaluation on IFC datasets, such as single-storey sample building models with manually curated question-answer pairs, demonstrates the feasibility of LLM-driven question answering over engineering data. Such systems can handle queries about building components ("Which walls contain fire-rated materials?"), spatial relationships ("What rooms are adjacent to the stairwell?"), and material specifications ("What is the thermal conductivity of the exterior wall assembly?").

Challenges specific to this domain are substantial. IFC schemas are large and deeply nested, with hundreds of entity types and complex inheritance hierarchies that exceed the schema complexity typical of general-purpose knowledge graphs. Domain-specific terminology—spanning structural engineering, HVAC systems, and building codes—requires precise attribute-level reasoning rather than approximate semantic matching. Property values often involve units, tolerances, and enumerated types that demand exact handling. Furthermore, the flexible and extensible nature of IFC (through property sets and quantity sets) means that equivalent information may be encoded differently across models, complicating schema-aware query generation.

The development of larger domain-specific datasets for IFC-linked graph queries remains an important direction. Current evaluation relies on small, manually curated benchmarks that cannot capture the full diversity of real-world engineering queries or the variability of IFC implementations across software vendors. Bridging the gap between general-purpose AI systems and real-world engineering applications requires not only expanded training data but also domain-grounded evaluation frameworks that assess correctness against engineering semantics rather than surface-level string matching.

## See Also

- [[z20260318-007|Labeled Property Graphs, Graph Query Languages, and Multi-Agent Query Generation]] — grounds the graph data model and query generation mechanisms that underlie IFC-based digital twin querying, with multi-agent text-to-Cypher workflows directly applicable to handling the schema complexity and entity verification challenges specific to IFC property graphs
- [[z20260318-008|Text-to-SQL and Natural Language Interfaces to Databases]] — contrasts relational approaches to natural language database access with the graph-native querying required for IFC data, where hierarchical building structures and typed spatial relationships resist flattening into tabular schemas
- [[z20260318-006|Retrieval-Augmented Generation and GraphRAG]] — applies graph-guided retrieval and graph-enhanced generation to the construction domain, where IFC-derived property graphs serve as the structured retrieval source for engineering question answering pipelines
